mcqs.question: In the Interval Scheduling problem, which greedy criterion leads to an optimal solution?

options.text: Select interval with earliest start time
Select interval with earliest finish time
Select interval with latest start time

mcqs.explanation: The earliest finish time greedy criterion is optimal. By selecting the interval that finishes earliest, we leave maximum room for subsequent intervals, maximizing the total count.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the greedy algorithm for Interval Scheduling?
mcqs.explanation: The algorithm requires sorting intervals by finish time (O(n log n)) followed by a single pass to select compatible intervals (O(n)), giving O(n log n) overall.

mcqs.question: In Fractional Knapsack, items are sorted by which criterion?
mcqs.explanation: Items are sorted by their value-to-weight ratio in descending order to prioritize items that give maximum value per unit of weight.

mcqs.question: Which of the following problems can be solved optimally using greedy approach?
mcqs.explanation: Only Fractional Knapsack can be solved optimally with greedy. 0/1 Knapsack requires dynamic programming because the greedy choice property does not hold.

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of Fractional Knapsack algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: The algorithm only requires a few variables for tracking current weight, total value, and indices, giving O(1) space complexity after sorting.

mcqs.question: In Interval Scheduling, two intervals are compatible if:

options.text: They have the same start time
One ends before or at the start of the other
They have the same finish time

mcqs.explanation: Compatible intervals do not overlap. Interval i finishes at or before interval j starts (fi ≤ sj), or vice versa.

mcqs.question: The greedy algorithm for Fractional Knapsack is optimal because of:

options.text: Both greedy choice property and optimal substructure

mcqs.explanation: Both properties are essential. Greedy choice property allows local optimal decisions, and optimal substructure ensures these lead to global optimum.

mcqs.question: What type of items can be taken in Fractional Knapsack but not in 0/1 Knapsack?

options.text: Any fraction of an item (0 to 1)

mcqs.explanation: Fractional knapsack allows taking any fraction (0 to 1) of an item, while 0/1 knapsack requires either taking the whole item or not taking it at all.

mcqs.question: In the Interval Scheduling algorithm, after sorting by finish time, what is the second step?

options.text: Select first interval, then iterate selecting intervals that start after last finish

mcqs.explanation: After sorting, we select the first interval (earliest finish), then iterate through remaining intervals, selecting each if its start time is >= last selected finish time.

mcqs.question: Which algorithm paradigm is used to solve Interval Scheduling and Fractional Knapsack?
mcqs.explanation: Both problems are classic examples of greedy algorithms, where local optimal choices lead to globally optimal solutions.

mcqs.question: What happens in Fractional Knapsack when the next item doesn't fit completely?
mcqs.explanation: We take only the fractional part that fits in the remaining capacity, filling the knapsack completely and maximizing total value.

mcqs.question: The proof of correctness for Interval Scheduling typically uses:
mcqs.explanation: The exchange argument shows that any optimal solution can be transformed to include the earliest-finishing interval without reducing the number of intervals.

mcqs.question: If all intervals have the same finish time in Interval Scheduling, which greedy choice is optimal?

options.text: Select interval with earliest start time among those with same finish
Select interval with latest start time

mcqs.explanation: When finish times are equal, selecting the interval with earliest start time (shortest duration) leaves maximum time before, which is optimal.

mcqs.question: For Fractional Knapsack with items (value, weight): (60, 10), (100, 20), (120, 30) and capacity 50, what is the optimal solution value?
mcqs.explanation: Ratios: 60/10=6, 100/20=5, 120/30=4. Take all of item1 (value 60), all of item2 (value 100), and 20/30 of item3 (value 80). Total = 60+100+80=280

mcqs.question: Which statement is TRUE about 0/1 Knapsack vs Fractional Knapsack?

options.text: Both have O(n log n) greedy solution
0/1 can be solved with greedy, Fractional cannot
Fractional can be solved with greedy, 0/1 cannot

mcqs.explanation: Fractional Knapsack has an optimal greedy solution (O(n log n)), while 0/1 Knapsack requires dynamic programming (O(nW)) as greedy fails.

mcqs.question: The main difference between 0/1 and Fractional Knapsack is:
mcqs.explanation: The key difference is that Fractional Knapsack allows taking fractions of items (dividibility), while 0/1 requires binary choice (whole item or nothing).

mcqs.question: In Interval Scheduling with intervals: [1,4], [2,3], [3,5], [4,6], how many maximum non-overlapping intervals can be selected?
mcqs.explanation: Sorted by finish: [2,3], [1,4], [3,5], [4,6]. Select [2,3], then [4,6]. Cannot select [1,4] or [3,5] as they overlap. Maximum = 2 intervals.

mcqs.question: Greedy algorithms guarantee optimal solution when:

options.text: When problem has greedy choice property and optimal substructure

mcqs.explanation: Greedy algorithms work when the problem exhibits both greedy choice property (local optimum leads to global optimum) and optimal substructure.

mcqs.question: What is the output of Fractional Knapsack when all items have the same value-to-weight ratio?

options.text: Must take items in order of weight

mcqs.explanation: When all ratios are equal, any combination that fills the capacity equally gives the same total value, so any greedy selection is optimal.

mcqs.question: Which data structure is commonly used to implement Interval Scheduling greedy algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: We need to sort intervals by finish time, so either a priority queue (min-heap by finish time) or sorting the array initially is used.
